original_query: SELECT * FROM posts LEFT JOIN topic_allowed_groups AS tg ON posts.topic_id = tg.topic_id WHERE tg.id IS NULL AND posts.topic_id IN (SELECT tau.topic_id FROM topic_allowed_users AS tau INNER JOIN topic_allowed_users AS tau2 ON tau2.topic_id = tau.topic_id AND tau2.id <> tau.id WHERE tau.user_id = 1086 GROUP BY tau.topic_id HAVING COUNT(*) = 1)
rewritten_query: SELECT posts.id, posts.title, posts.content FROM posts INNER JOIN topic_allowed_groups AS tg ON posts.topic_id = tg.topic_id WHERE tg.id IS NULL AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM topic_allowed_users AS tau WHERE tau.topic_id = posts.topic_id AND tau.user_id = 1086 GROUP BY tau.topic_id HAVING COUNT(*) = 1)
agent_analysis: The original query has a subquery that can be optimized. The subquery is performing a self-join on the 'topic_allowed_users' table, which can be costly. Additionally, using 'SELECT *' can lead to unnecessary data retrieval. The use of 'LEFT JOIN' may also lead to performance issues if the 'topic_allowed_groups' table is large and the condition 'tg.id IS NULL' is not selective enough.

original_query: SELECT * FROM posts WHERE (topic_id = 6020 AND post_number = 1) OR id IN (SELECT p1.id FROM posts AS p1 WHERE p1.percent_rank <= 88.0 AND p1.topic_id = 7290)
rewritten_query: SELECT p.* FROM posts p JOIN (SELECT p1.id FROM posts AS p1 WHERE p1.percent_rank <= 88.0 AND p1.topic_id = 7290) AS subquery ON p.id = subquery.id WHERE (p.topic_id = 6020 AND p.post_number = 1) OR p.id = subquery.id
agent_analysis: The original SQL query has a potential performance bottleneck due to the use of a subquery in the IN clause, which can be inefficient, especially if the subquery returns a large number of rows. Additionally, the lack of proper indexing on the columns used in the WHERE clause may lead to full table scans, increasing execution time. The logical condition in the WHERE clause could also lead to confusion in execution order, potentially affecting performance.

original_query: SELECT * FROM posts INNER JOIN topics ON topics.id = posts.topic_id WHERE posts.id NOT IN (SELECT post_id FROM post_search_data WHERE locale = '0')
rewritten_query: SELECT posts.id, posts.title, posts.content, topics.name FROM posts LEFT JOIN topics ON topics.id = posts.topic_id LEFT JOIN post_search_data ON posts.id = post_search_data.post_id AND post_search_data.locale = '0' WHERE post_search_data.post_id IS NULL
agent_analysis: The SQL query uses a subquery with 'NOT IN', which can lead to performance issues, especially if the subquery returns a large number of rows. original_query: SELECT * FROM category_users WHERE user_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM users)

original_query: SELECT * FROM notifications WHERE notifications.topic_id IS NULL OR notifications.topic_id IN (SELECT id FROM topics WHERE deleted_at IS NULL)
rewritten_query: SELECT n.* FROM notifications n LEFT JOIN topics t ON n.topic_id = t.id WHERE n.topic_id IS NULL OR (t.id IS NOT NULL AND t.deleted_at IS NULL)
agent_analysis: The query retrieves all columns from the notifications table where the topic_id is either NULL or matches IDs from the topics table that are not deleted. The use of 'SELECT *' can lead to performance issues, especially if the notifications table has many columns. original_query: SELECT categories.* FROM categories INNER JOIN topics ON categories.topic_id = topics.id WHERE categories.id = 1 OR (categories.parent_category_id = 1 AND categories.topic_id <> topics.id)
rewritten_query: SELECT categories.id, categories.name, categories.parent_category_id, categories.topic_id FROM categories INNER JOIN topics ON categories.topic_id = topics.id WHERE categories.id = 1 UNION ALL SELECT categories.id, categories.name, categories.parent_category_id, categories.topic_id FROM categories WHERE categories.parent_category_id = 1 AND categories.topic_id <> topics.id
agent_analysis: The query retrieves all columns from the 'categories' table and performs an INNER JOIN with the 'topics' table. original_query: SELECT issues.* FROM issues WHERE issues.project_id = 13083 AND issues.iid = 1 ORDER BY issues.id DESC LIMIT 1
rewritten_query: SELECT issues.id, issues.title, issues.description FROM issues WHERE issues.project_id = 13083 AND issues.iid = 1 ORDER BY issues.id DESC LIMIT 1
agent_analysis: The query retrieves all columns from the 'issues' table for a specific project and issue ID, ordering by 'id' in descending order. The use of 'ORDER BY' with 'LIMIT 1' can be inefficient if there are many rows to sort. original_query: SELECT ci_pipelines.* FROM ci_pipelines WHERE ci_pipelines.id IN (SELECT MAX(id) FROM ci_pipelines WHERE ci_pipelines.project_id = 14074169 AND ci_pipelines."ref" IN ('actually', 'existing', 'refs', 'here') GROUP BY ci_pipelines."ref") ORDER BY ci_pipelines.id DESC LIMIT 20 OFFSET 0
rewritten_query: WITH RankedPipelines AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY "ref" ORDER BY id DESC) as rn FROM ci_pipelines WHERE project_id = 14074169 AND "ref" IN ('actually', 'existing', 'refs', 'here')) SELECT * FROM RankedPipelines WHERE rn <= 20 ORDER BY id DESC
agent_analysis: The query uses a subquery to find the maximum id for each ref, which can be inefficient, especially if the ci_pipelines table is large. The use of IN with a subquery can lead to performance issues as it may not utilize indexes effectively. Additionally, the ORDER BY clause combined with LIMIT and OFFSET can lead to performance degradation when dealing with large datasets.

original_query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE users.state = 'active' AND (users.ghost = 0 OR users.ghost IS NULL) AND (users.bot_type = 0 OR users.bot_type IS NULL) AND (id IN (SELECT user_id FROM project_authorizations WHERE project_id = 13083 AND access_level > 20) OR id IN (443319)) AND id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM approvals WHERE merge_request_id = 2294769) AND users.id <> 840794

agent_analysis: The SQL query has several potential performance bottlenecks: 1) The use of 'IN' and 'NOT IN' subqueries can lead to performance issues, especially if the subqueries return a large number of rows. 2) The conditions on 'users.ghost' and 'users.bot_type' could be optimized by using a more efficient filtering method. 3) The query may benefit from proper indexing on the columns used in the WHERE clause and the subqueries.

original_query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ci_builds WHERE ci_builds.type = 'Ci::Build' AND ci_builds.id IN (SELECT MAX(ci_builds.id) FROM ci_builds WHERE ci_builds.type = 'Ci::Build' GROUP BY ci_builds.name, ci_builds.commit_id) AND ci_builds.status IN ('failed', 'canceled') AND ci_builds.commit_id = 8088

original_query: SELECT ci_builds.* FROM ci_builds WHERE ci_builds.type = 'Ci::Build' AND (artifacts_file <> '' OR EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ci_job_artifacts WHERE ci_builds.id = ci_job_artifacts.job_id))

original_query: SELECT tags.* FROM tags INNER JOIN (SELECT DISTINCT tag_id FROM taggings WHERE taggable_type = 'default') AS t ON tags.id = t.tag_id

original_query: SELECT spree_products.* FROM spree_products WHERE spree_products.deleted_at IS NULL AND spree_products.id IN (SELECT product_id FROM spree_variants WHERE count_on_hand > 0 GROUP BY product_id) LIMIT 100
rewritten_query: SELECT spree_products.* FROM spree_products WHERE spree_products.deleted_at IS NULL AND spree_products.id IN (SELECT product_id FROM spree_variants WHERE count_on_hand > 0) LIMIT 100

original_query: SELECT comments.* FROM comments WHERE comments.is_deleted = 0 AND comments.is_moderated = 0 AND comments.story_id NOT IN (SELECT story_id FROM hidden_stories WHERE user_id = 1) ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 20 OFFSET 0
